message:
harden home-directory resolution across every ACFS subsystem

Follow-up to 74f34eab ("expand passwd-based home resolution into every
ACFS subsystem"). That commit moved all subsystems onto a common pattern,
but left two lingering weaknesses: (1) HOME was still preferred over the
passwd database, so a clobbered HOME in a subshell or systemd context
would silently route state into the wrong directory, and (2) id/whoami/
getent were resolved via PATH, which breaks under restricted or
compromised environments where a shim can poison user/home lookups.

This commit closes both gaps everywhere.

Prefer passwd-based home over $HOME
-----------------------------------
Every *_resolve_current_home() helper now consults the passwd database
first and only falls back to a sanitized $HOME when passwd lookup fails.
Previously the order was reversed, which meant any subsystem invoked
with a bogus/unset/stale HOME would happily return that bogus value.
Now the authoritative source (passwd) wins, and $HOME is only a last
resort for environments without a usable passwd backend.

Touched in 15 subsystems with identical shape:
  scripts/lib/doctor.sh
  scripts/lib/doctor_fix.sh
  scripts/lib/notifications.sh
  scripts/lib/notify.sh
  scripts/lib/webhook.sh
  scripts/lib/state.sh
  scripts/lib/changelog.sh
  scripts/lib/cheatsheet.sh
  scripts/lib/continue.sh
  scripts/lib/dashboard.sh
  scripts/lib/export-config.sh
  scripts/lib/info.sh
  scripts/lib/nightly_update.sh
  scripts/lib/smoke_test.sh
  scripts/lib/status.sh
  scripts/lib/support.sh
  scripts/services-setup.sh
  scripts/preflight.sh
  packages/onboard/onboard.sh

PATH-independent system binary resolution
-----------------------------------------
Each subsystem now exposes three matching helpers:
  <ns>_system_binary_path    — resolve id/whoami/getent from a fixed
                               allow-list of absolute paths
                               (/usr/bin, /bin, /usr/sbin, /sbin)
  <ns>_resolve_current_user  — id -un -> whoami fallback, both via
                               the absolute-path resolver above
  <ns>_getent_passwd_entry   — getent passwd <user> via the absolute
                               resolver

The previous `id -un 2>/dev/null || whoami 2>/dev/null` idiom relied on
PATH and inherited shell state; the replacement cannot be redirected
by PATH manipulation and degrades cleanly if one of the binaries is
absent. This matters for acfs-update/acfs-global which can be invoked
from minimal systemd units or from mid-install bootstraps where PATH
may be intentionally narrowed.

acfs-global HOME validation guard
---------------------------------
scripts/acfs-global gains current_home_matches_acfs_install(), a
defense-in-depth check used when resolve_current_home() would otherwise
accept a user-supplied $HOME. It now requires the candidate home to
either:
  - contain a valid .acfs/state.json AND a discoverable acfs bin AND
    have a state-recorded target_user that matches the current user,
    OR
  - contain no ACFS install artefacts at all.

If the candidate $HOME contains .acfs state but the state does not
match the current user, resolve_current_home() refuses it rather than
silently proceeding with the wrong install context. find_acfs_bin()
likewise now consults TARGET_HOME through validated_target_user_for_
dispatch before falling back to passwd. resolve_direct_exec_target_home
also prefers an explicit TARGET_HOME over state-hint probing, so the
precedence from the outer wrapper is preserved end-to-end.

scripts/acfs-update mirrors the same changes.

Early TARGET_HOME honoring in update.sh
---------------------------------------
scripts/lib/update.sh now pre-seats HOME from TARGET_HOME at source
time (before any helper runs), via _update_initial_existing_home().
This fixes a long-standing race where update.sh's own sanitization
ran against an inherited HOME before the target-home plumbing took
effect, occasionally causing update operations to target the wrong
user's ~/.acfs during multi-user sudo dispatch.

Also adds update_runtime_shell_home() which refuses to resolve when
TARGET_USER is set but does not match the current user — preventing
cleanup_legacy_git_safety_guard() from ever operating in the wrong
user's hooks dir.

Test coverage
-------------
tests/unit/lib/test_update.bats and tests/unit/test_changelog_export_
status.sh grow substantial new cases covering:
  - passwd-preferred resolution under a clobbered $HOME
  - absolute-path binary resolution with a poisoned PATH
  - TARGET_HOME/TARGET_USER cross-user refusal
  - acfs-global install-matching guard on mismatched state.json
  - update.sh early TARGET_HOME honoring

+onboard_system_binary_path() {
+    local name="${1:-}"
+    local candidate=""
+
+    [[ -n "$name" ]] || return 1
+
+    for candidate in \
+        "/usr/bin/$name" \
+        "/bin/$name" \
+        "/usr/sbin/$name" \
+        "/sbin/$name"
+    do
+        [[ -x "$candidate" ]] || continue
+        printf '%s\n' "$candidate"
+        return 0
+    done
+
+    return 1
+}
+
+onboard_resolve_current_user() {
+    local current_user=""
+    local id_bin=""
+    local whoami_bin=""
+
+    id_bin="$(onboard_system_binary_path id 2>/dev/null || true)"
+    if [[ -n "$id_bin" ]]; then
+        current_user="$("$id_bin" -un 2>/dev/null || true)"
+    fi
+
+    if [[ -z "$current_user" ]]; then
+        whoami_bin="$(onboard_system_binary_path whoami 2>/dev/null || true)"
+        if [[ -n "$whoami_bin" ]]; then
+            current_user="$("$whoami_bin" 2>/dev/null || true)"
+        fi
+    fi
+
+    [[ -n "$current_user" ]] || return 1
+    printf '%s\n' "$current_user"
+}
+
+onboard_getent_passwd_entry() {
+    local user="${1:-}"
+    local getent_bin=""
+
+    getent_bin="$(onboard_system_binary_path getent 2>/dev/null || true)"
+    [[ -n "$getent_bin" ]] || return 1
+
+    if [[ -n "$user" ]]; then
+        "$getent_bin" passwd "$user" 2>/dev/null
+    else
+        "$getent_bin" passwd 2>/dev/null
+    fi
+}

     local user="$1"

+    if [[ -z "$passwd_entry" ]] && [[ -r /etc/passwd ]]; then
         passwd_entry="$(awk -F: -v u="$user" '$1==u{print $0; exit}' /etc/passwd 2>/dev/null || true)"
     fi
